title: When to replace a gas boiler: the timing decision
subtitle: Age, repair costs, EPC implications, and the path to low-carbon heating
excerpt: Most gas boilers last 15-20 years. The decision to repair or replace depends on age, repair cost relative to replacement cost, and whether the next heating system will be a gas boiler again or a heat pump.
sourceNotes: HHIC (Heating and Hot Water Industry Council) boiler lifetime data. Boiler Upgrade Scheme gov.uk guidance for current BUS grant levels. Energy Saving Trust heat pump decision guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A device that moves heat from a low-temperature source (outside air or the ground) to a higher-temperature heat emitter circuit using a refrigerant cycle and electrical energy. At a flow temperature of 35 degrees C an air-source heat pump typically delivers 3-4 kWh of heat for every 1 kWh of electricity used.

Energy Performance Certificate: a certificate rating a building's energy efficiency from A to G. Required at sale or let in the UK, and used as a reference point for retrofit grant eligibility and minimum energy efficiency standards.
Boiler Upgrade Scheme: a UK government grant toward heat pump installation. From April 2022, the grant is 7,500 pounds for an air-source heat pump in England and Wales.

content.text: A gas boiler that is more than 12 years old is approaching the end of its typical service life. The standard repair-or-replace decision involves comparing the repair cost against the expected remaining lifetime of the boiler. But for a boiler that will be replaced with a
heat pump
, the decision is different: the transition to low-carbon heating requires the house to be ready, not just the boiler to have failed.
The standard repair-or-replace calculation

The rule of thumb from the heating industry: if the repair cost exceeds 50 percent of the replacement cost of a new boiler, replace rather than repair. For a standard combi boiler, replacement costs range from around 1,500 to 3,000 pounds installed. On this rule, a repair costing more than 750-1,500 pounds on a boiler over 10 years old points to replacement.

The calculation changes with age. A repair costing 400 pounds on a 6-year-old boiler is worth doing. The same repair on a 16-year-old boiler represents spending money to extend the life of a failing system by one to two years at most.
The heat pump timing question
The
BUS
grant of 7,500 pounds (England and Wales, 2026 figure) is available for heat pump installations replacing any fossil fuel heating system. To qualify, the home must have an
EPC
that is not rated F or G. If the EPC is below D, the installer may recommend basic insulation measures before the heat pump installation to ensure the system will perform adequately.

The case for replacing early: a boiler that fails in January forces an emergency swap under time pressure. Replacing now allows time to get an EPC, insulate, size the heat pump, and pick an installer. It also keeps the BUS grant available; an emergency gas boiler swap forfeits it.

The case against replacing early: replacing a working boiler incurs an immediate cost that could have been deferred. If the house is not ready for a heat pump (EPC F or G, major insulation work needed), another gas boiler may be the practical choice while the house is brought up to standard.
The EPC and the heat pump calculation

A heat pump running a poorly insulated house with old radiators at a flow temperature of 55 degrees C will have a SCOP of around 2.0-2.2. At current electricity prices of 27p/kWh and gas at 6p/kWh, a SCOP of 2.0 means the heat pump costs 27 / 2 = 13.5p per useful kWh of heat, versus 6 / 0.9 = 6.7p for a condensing boiler at 90 percent efficiency. This is worse than the gas boiler. The heat pump advantage only emerges when the SCOP rises above 4.0, which requires flow temperatures around 35 degrees C.

Typical prep work: loft insulation to 270 mm, cavity fill if accessible, sealing draughts around doors and windows, and larger radiators sized for 40-45 degrees C flow. The installer will calculate the heat demand and size the radiators before fitting the pump.
Interim options

Carry out the insulation work now with the existing boiler still running. Once the heat demand has fallen, the radiators that were marginal at low flow temperatures become adequate and the house is ready for a heat pump when the boiler needs replacing.

Lower the boiler flow temperature now. Many condensing boilers can operate at 55 degrees C rather than the default 70 degrees C with no modification. Running at lower flow temperatures increases the boiler's condensing efficiency and cuts gas consumption by 5-10 percent.
Scotland and Wales

Scotland has Home Energy Scotland Loan and Grant rather than BUS; levels and criteria differ. Wales aligns with England on most grants but is administered separately. Northern Ireland has its own rules. If the property is not in England, check the current grant position with the relevant national energy agency before deciding.
